story.title: Introducing Parallel Agents in Zed - Zed Blog

comments.text: I've been using Zed since the early days and this parallel agents feature finally makes me feel like I'm working with an actual IDE instead of a fancy editor. Reminds me of how VS Code's extension model took off once they nailed the UX for multiple tools running at once. The 120

kids.text: The 120 fps thing is legitimately the standout here. I've been switching between Zed and VS Code weekly, and the difference in responsiveness when you have multiple agents running is night and day. Scrolling through a file while an agent is refactoring in another thread doesn't stutter

Have you tried Cursor? Their parallel agent orchestration actually ships faster iteration loops because they handle context window management differently. The 120 fps is nice but if agents are blocking on token generation anyway, the UX gains flatten pretty quick.

comments.text:
The 120 fps rendering is the real story here. Most agent IDEs I've tried (including some very well-funded ones) tank to 30-40 fps the moment you have 2-3 threads running with language server overhead. Zed's architecture of keeping that smooth even with parallel agents is genuinely non-trivial.

That said, the Threads Sidebar layout change feels like it's solving a UI problem that shouldn't exist in the first place. You're swapping which side panels live on, but the fundamental issue remains: agents writing code across multiple repos need better visibility into diffs and merge conflicts. Just moving the sidebar left doesn't really address that. Still waiting to see how this handles the case where two parallel agents try to edit the same file simultaneously - the blog doesn't mention it and that's the scenario that breaks most tools.

kids.text:
The 120 fps thing is real, but I'd push back on the framing a bit. Zed's smooth rendering under agent load isn't magic architecture so much as ruthless UI discipline. They built the editor first, optimized rendering second, then bolted agents on top. Most "well-funded" IDEs you mention did the reverse: took a web stack (Electron, VS Code's architecture), slapped agents on it, and hoped for the best.

That said, the parent's point about the Threads Sidebar solving a non-problem stands. This feels like rearranging deckchairs. The real issue with multi-agent workflows isn't which panel is on which side-it's that you need completely different mental models depending on whether agents are working in isolation, collaboratively, or sequentially. Cursor tried to solve this with the Composer UX, but it's still fundamentally a single-thread metaphor with visual chrome.

What would actually move the needle: per-thread context windows, so one agent's work doesn't pollute another's state.

This reminds me of the old Smalltalk IDEs from the 80s/90s, which had similar multi-window agent-like workflows. The UI patterns there actually solved the "which panel goes where" problem by letting you tile windows freely instead of docking everything to fixed sides. Wonder if

The real issue here is that Zed is solving a problem that only exists because the industry decided agents needed IDE integration in the first place. A year ago we were fine with Claude in a browser tab and a terminal. Now every editor vendor is racing to embed agentic workflows directly into their UI, and we're calling it progress.

The 120 fps thing is legitimately impressive from a rendering standpoint. But it's also a distraction from the fact that we're optimizing the wrong thing. We should be asking whether parallel agents in an editor window is actually the workflow developers want, not just whether we can make it smooth.

The Threads Sidebar is a band-aid on a deeper UX problem: agents are fundamentally interrupting and context-switching heavy, and moving panels around doesn't fix that. It just makes the interruption prettier.

comments.text: Parallel agents in an editor is a solution looking for a problem. Most developers don't need five things running at once in their IDE. This feels like feature creep to justify "AI-native" positioning rather than something people actually asked for.

kids.text: Tried this workflow yesterday with Claude and Sonnet running parallel on a medium Rails project. Stopped one agent mid-refactor, spun up another to handle database migrations in a separate thread while the first kept going. The sidebar made it actually usable-without it, toggling between agents would've been

The Rails refactor use case makes sense, but I'd push back on the framing. This isn't "five things at once"-it's task isolation. One agent handling schema changes while another rewrites controllers in parallel, each with its own context window and access rules via the sidebar. That's genuinely

comments.text: This is solid, but Cursor's parallel agents feel snappier for quick refactors. The UX there is more "fire and forget" whereas Zed's Threads Sidebar seems optimized for observation. Both approaches work, just depends if you want to babysit or delegate.

kids.text: "Fire and forget" doesn't really apply when your agent hallucinates and commits broken code to main. Cursor's UX might feel snappier in the demo, but I'd want visibility into what's actually running, which is what the Threads Sidebar gives you. The observation overhead is the

Remember when we actually tested software before shipping it? Cursor's "fire and forget" is just a prettier name for "hope it works". I'd rather sit and watch than explain to my team why main is broken at 5pm. The Threads Sidebar is the first agent UI that respects the fact that these things still hallucinate constantly.

comments.text:
The interesting bit here is that Zed is positioning parallel agents as this revolutionary IDE feature, but VS Code's been doing multi-terminal layouts with agent tools (Claude in VSCode, GitHub Copilot Chat, etc.) for years now. The difference seems to be that Zed baked it into the UI layer rather than treating agents as plugins.

The real gotcha: once you're running 4-5 agents in parallel across different repos, who's responsible when they conflict? If agent A modifies file X while agent B is reading it, or worse, they both try to write simultaneously, does Zed handle that, or are you managing merge conflicts manually? The blog post doesn't

kids.text: I actually tested this with 3 agents running simultaneously in Zed yesterday, and yeah, the 120 fps hold-up is real. Feels snappier than VS Code with multiple extensions doing the same work. But the conflict resolution you're asking about? I ran into it immediately. Agent A

Stop thinking about this as a conflict resolution problem and start thinking about it as a concurrency model problem. Zed's framing agents as tools that modify files, but what they should be doing is exposing a diff-based workflow where agents propose changes, you review them in parallel, then you merge or reject
